trust_assumption: An auto-remediation/SOAR playbook treats alert fields it ingests (hostname, username, file path, URL, process command line, IOC) as already-sanitized parameters, safe to interpolate directly into the API calls, shell actions, and enrichment queries it fires without an analyst in the loop.
violation: An attacker who controls a value that lands in an alert field (a username, a filename, a User-Agent, a DNS label) plants payload that the playbook expands into the remediation action itself, turning the defender's response automation into attacker-directed command/API execution against the very assets it was meant to protect.

techniques.name: Alert-field to playbook-action injection (host isolation, account disable, firewall rule) via attacker-controlled telemetry
Enrichment-query injection turning SOAR API tokens into attacker-scoped calls

rationale: SOC teams adopted no-touch SOAR/auto-remediation faster than they hardened the data path into it: the playbook author assumes alert fields are trustworthy because they came from the EDR/SIEM, but those fields are populated from attacker-influenced artifacts (process names, file paths, web logs). The defender's own privileged automation runner now executes against parameters the adversary chose, and because the action is a 'response' it is allowlisted, unlogged as suspicious, and runs with broad cross-platform tokens. There is no public tooling that fuzzes the alert-field-to-action expansion and almost no detection that treats a remediation action as a potential injection sink.
suggested_research: Build a harness that seeds malicious values into observable artifact types (filename, username, UA, DNS) and traces which fields reach SOAR action templates unescaped in Splunk SOAR/Sentinel/Tines/Cortex XSOAR; propose taint-tracking from telemetry ingestion to playbook action sinks.

trust_assumption: A SOC's detection-as-code pipeline trusts that a Sigma/YARA/Sentinel rule merged from the shared community or vendor rule repository, having passed CI lint and review, faithfully covers the TTP its title and metadata claim to detect.
violation: An attacker who lands a subtly over-scoped filter, a broken field mapping, or a logic-narrowing exception into the shared rule corpus ships a detection that looks present and green in coverage dashboards but silently excludes the exact behavior the adversary intends to run, blinding every downstream SOC that syncs the repo.

techniques.name: Poisoned Sigma/detection rule with coverage-preserving exclusion clause via PR to shared repo
Field-mapping/log-source desync that renders a rule non-firing while reporting as enabled

rationale: Detection-as-code moved the SOC from hand-tuned rules to git-synced community rule repos (Sigma HQ, Elastic detection-rules, Sentinel content hub) reviewed by humans who check syntax and intent prose, not adversarial coverage. A reviewer cannot easily tell that an added `filter` or a renamed field reduces true-positive coverage to zero while the rule still parses, deploys, and shows green in MITRE-coverage heatmaps. This is a provenance seam: the SOC trusts the rule for where it came from, not for what it actually matches against real events. No tooling differentially tests a rule's firing surface before/after a PR, and the blind spot persists unnoticed until the excluded TTP is used.
suggested_research: Build a regression harness that replays a labeled event corpus against rule PRs and flags coverage deltas; mine Sigma/Elastic repo history for exclusion clauses correlated with known TTPs and measure mean time-to-detect a deliberately weakening PR.

trust_assumption: A cloud-delivered EDR/UEBA detection model trusts that the streamed endpoint telemetry it continuously trains its behavioral baseline on reflects honest, representative normal activity for that host or identity, so a behavior that fits the learned baseline is benign by construction.
violation: An attacker who has low-and-slow presence drip-feeds the malicious behavior into the telemetry stream at a rate below the model's anomaly threshold, shifting the rolling baseline until the eventual full-tempo action sits inside the now-normalized envelope and never scores as anomalous.

techniques.name: Slow-drip behavioral baseline shifting against cloud UEBA/EDR ML scoring
Adversarial concept-drift induction to widen the learned normal envelope

rationale: Vendors moved detection from static signatures to continuously-retraining cloud behavioral baselines (UEBA, EDR ML) far faster than they built integrity controls on the training stream. The check-to-use gap is the model's whole adaptation window: behavior that is anomalous on day one becomes baseline on day thirty if introduced gradually, and the defender has no notion that the baseline was steered rather than learned. Unlike classic evasion (hiding from a fixed rule), this attacks the model's plasticity. There is no public tooling to measure how much an adversary can move a given vendor's baseline per unit time, and no detection distinguishes organic drift from deliberate drift.
suggested_research: Empirically characterize the per-feature drift rate that stays under-threshold for leading UEBA products; design a poisoning-rate estimator and propose drift-provenance anchoring (immutable early-baseline reference) as a control.

trust_assumption: A relying party trusts a synced FIDO2/passkey assertion as proof of a phishing-resistant hardware-bound authenticator, even though the private key is replicated by the platform vendor's cloud (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, vendor passkey sync) across every device in the user's account and recoverable through that account.
violation: An attacker who compromises the platform account or its recovery path (or downgrades a passkey-enrolled account back to weaker fallback factors) obtains or re-provisions the synced credential off-device, defeating the RP's assumption of device possession without ever touching the user's hardware.

techniques.name: Passkey sync-fabric account takeover to export/replicate the synced credential
Account-recovery downgrade to fallback factor that bypasses the enrolled passkey

rationale: The industry pushed passkeys hard as phishing-resistant and 'hardware-bound', but the consumer reality is synced passkeys whose security collapses to the security of the platform cloud account and, worse, to whatever recovery/fallback factor the RP left enabled for account-recovery UX. Defenders treat a passkey assertion's AAGUID/UV flag as proof of a strong authenticator and rarely inspect whether the credential is sync-eligible or whether a weaker recovery path still exists. The provenance seam: the RP checks the assertion's claimed strength, not where the key actually lives. Tooling to enumerate sync-eligibility and recovery-downgrade paths per RP is nascent and detection of off-device credential re-provisioning is absent.
suggested_research: Map major RPs' acceptance of synced vs device-bound credentials and their residual recovery factors; build a tool that flags RPs accepting sync-eligible passkeys while still permitting SMS/email recovery, quantifying the downgrade surface.

trust_assumption: A conditional-access / zero-trust IdP grants or relaxes access based on a device-compliance and posture signal (managed, patched, EDR-healthy, disk-encrypted) that it treats as a trustworthy attestation, when much of that posture is computed and asserted by a client-side agent the user's device fully controls.
violation: An attacker on the device tampers with the posture agent's reported state or replays a known-good attestation, presenting a non-compliant or compromised endpoint as healthy so the IdP applies the trusted-device policy branch and waives step-up controls.

techniques.name: Client-asserted device-compliance signal forgery against conditional access
Posture-attestation replay / hooking of the compliance agent

rationale: Zero-trust adoption made device posture a primary access gate, but the posture is frequently client-asserted (an agent reports 'compliant') rather than hardware-rooted, and even where a TPM is involved the binding between the attested boot state and the live runtime posture is weak. The IdP authorizes the asserted position, not verified ground truth. Defenders display posture as authoritative in dashboards and gate sensitive apps on it, yet there is little public tooling that systematically forges or replays each vendor's compliance signal, and almost no detection that cross-checks asserted posture against independent server-side observation. Defense automated the gate faster than it rooted the signal.
suggested_research: Inventory which posture attributes per major MDM/IdP (Intune, Jamf, Okta Device Trust) are client-asserted vs hardware-attested; build a posture-spoofing test agent and propose server-corroborated posture as a control.

trust_assumption: An artifact verifier (admission controller, deploy gate, package consumer) trusts a valid SLSA/in-toto provenance attestation as proof that the artifact was built as described, treating a cryptographically verified, correctly-signed attestation as covering the entire build that produced what is being deployed.
violation: An attacker exploits the gap between what the attestation actually binds and what mattered: a pre-build step, a vendored dependency, a post-build mutation, or a subject-digest scope the predicate never covered, so a genuinely signed and verifiable attestation vouches for an artifact whose malicious portion was outside its claim.

techniques.name: Provenance scope gap (attested build step != exploited step) passing verification
Subject/predicate digest-binding mismatch letting an unattested artifact ride a valid attestation

rationale: The ecosystem rushed to 'verify provenance' (Sigstore, SLSA, in-toto, GitHub artifact attestations) and admission controllers now gate on a green signature check. But verification proves the attestation is authentic and well-formed, not that its scope covers the step the attacker abused: dependency resolution, base-image selection, a hermeticity gap, or a digest that binds the wrong subject. Defenders read 'provenance verified' as 'artifact trustworthy', a provenance seam where the receiver checks the label, not the thing. Public tooling to audit the coverage gap between an attestation's predicate and the real build DAG is nascent, and nothing detects a deploy that passed verification while the exploited step lay outside the claim.
suggested_research: Survey real SLSA/in-toto predicates in the wild for which build steps they actually bind; build a scope-coverage linter that diffs the attested DAG against the full build and flags unattested influence on the final digest.

trust_assumption: A relying party releasing secrets to a confidential-computing workload trusts a TEE remote-attestation quote as proof that the enclave is genuine and running unmodified, trusted code on a patched TCB, treating a quote that verifies against the vendor's roots as current and complete.
violation: An attacker exploits the gap between when the TCB was measured/attested and when secrets are used, or relies on a TCB still vulnerable to a known issue that triggered a security-version (TCB recovery) update the verifier failed to enforce, replaying a stale-but-valid quote to a verifier that does not check freshness or minimum SVN.

techniques.name: Stale TEE attestation replay against a verifier missing freshness/nonce binding
TCB-recovery / minimum-SVN downgrade accepted by lax appraisal policy

rationale: Confidential computing (SGX/TDX/SEV-SNP, Azure/GCP confidential VMs, confidential AI inference) is being adopted to release secrets and data to enclaves, and verifiers commonly accept a quote that chains to the vendor root without enforcing nonce freshness or a minimum security-version after a TCB recovery event. The check-to-use gap is real: a quote proves a measurement at issuance, not the live state at secret use, and TCB-recovery enforcement is an appraisal-policy choice operators routinely get wrong. Defenders treat 'attestation passed' as binary trust. Tooling that audits a verifier's freshness and SVN-minimum policy is nascent, and there is no detection for a relying party releasing keys against a stale or under-versioned quote.
suggested_research: Audit common attestation-verification libraries and managed services for default nonce-freshness and minimum-SVN enforcement; build a test that replays an old valid quote and a below-recovery SVN to measure which verifiers accept them.

trust_assumption: An eBPF-based security sensor trusts the kernel event stream it observes (and the order/scope of the hook points it attaches) as a complete, tamper-evident view of system activity, and the kernel trusts a privileged, verifier-passing eBPF program loaded by a capable principal as benign instrumentation.
violation: An attacker who can load eBPF (CAP_BPF/privileged container) or who operates in the gaps between the sensor's hook points uses eBPF itself to filter, mask, or rewrite the very events the defensive eBPF tool relies on, or attaches before/below it, so the security sensor inherits a view its own substrate silently edited.

techniques.name: eBPF-as-rootkit: hook-ordering and map tampering to hide processes/connections from eBPF EDR
Coverage blind spot between sensor hook points (syscall variants, uprobes, ringbuf gaps)

rationale: eBPF became the default substrate for modern runtime security (Falco, Cilium Tetragon, vendor eBPF EDR) much faster than the question 'who watches the eBPF layer' was answered. The defensive tool inherits the trustworthiness of a kernel surface that another privileged eBPF program (or a capable container) can manipulate, and the sensor's coverage is only as complete as its chosen hook set, with documented gaps. This is a context-inheritance seam: the sensor inherits the kernel's view without re-validating that nothing edited it. Offensive eBPF rootkit research exists but mature tooling targeting specific eBPF EDRs is nascent, and there is essentially no detection of eBPF-on-eBPF tampering or hook-ordering attacks.
suggested_research: Enumerate the hook points each major eBPF security tool attaches and map the syscall/event gaps; prototype an eBPF program that masks events from a co-resident eBPF sensor and propose attach-time integrity/ordering attestation.

trust_assumption: A cloud secrets broker / workload-identity-federation trust trusts that an OIDC token from a configured external issuer, whose subject/audience claims match the trust policy, was minted for the specific workload the policy intends, treating a claim-matching token as a faithful binding to that exact non-human principal.
violation: An attacker obtains or causes issuance of a token from the same trusted issuer whose claims satisfy an over-broad or wildcarded subject/audience condition (a different repo, branch, namespace, or service account under the same issuer), assuming the federated identity and exchanging it for cloud credentials the policy never meant to grant.

techniques.name: Workload identity federation subject/audience over-scope (wildcard repo/branch/namespace) credential exchange
Trusted-issuer token misbinding across workloads sharing an OIDC provider

rationale: To kill long-lived secrets, teams adopted keyless workload identity federation (GitHub OIDC to AWS/GCP/Azure, GKE/EKS workload identity, SPIFFE) at scale, but the security collapses to the precision of the trust-policy claim conditions, which are routinely wildcarded for convenience (any branch, any repo in an org, any pod in a namespace). The broker authorizes the position the claim names, not the intent, so a token from a less-trusted sibling workload under the same issuer is accepted. Defenders see 'no static keys' as the win and rarely audit subject-condition tightness. Tooling that enumerates over-broad federation bindings is nascent, and detection of a token-claim-vs-intended-workload mismatch at exchange time is essentially absent.
suggested_research: Build a scanner that parses IAM/role trust policies and federation configs to flag wildcarded subject/audience conditions and ranks blast radius; correlate with which sibling workloads can actually mint a satisfying token.

trust_assumption: A service in a mesh trusts that because a request arrived over mutual-TLS bearing a verified SPIFFE/workload identity from a sidecar, the L7 content of that request (path, method, headers, propagated JWT, on-behalf-of claims) was authorized for that caller, conflating transport-level peer authentication with application-level authorization.
violation: A compromised or over-privileged in-mesh workload that holds a valid mTLS identity sends requests whose L7 semantics it is not entitled to (privileged paths, spoofed forwarded-identity headers, replayed propagated tokens), and downstream services that gate only on the authenticated peer identity grant access the L7 policy should have denied.

techniques.name: Authenticated-peer to L7 authz confusion (privileged path/method abuse inside the mesh)
Forwarded-identity / propagated-JWT header spoof between meshed services

rationale: Service meshes (Istio, Linkerd, Consul) made mTLS-everywhere cheap, and teams adopted it as the security story, but mTLS proves who the peer is, not what the peer may do at L7. Many meshes default to peer-authn with permissive or absent fine-grained authorization policy, and forwarded-identity headers are trusted inside the trust boundary. A single compromised workload inherits the mesh-wide ability to reach services its application-layer entitlements never granted. This is a context-inheritance seam: downstream inherits the transport's authenticated trust without re-validating L7 intent. Tooling to map mesh authz gaps and forge forwarded-identity is nascent, and detection of intra-mesh L7 authz violations is rare because the traffic is encrypted-and-trusted.
suggested_research: Build a mesh-authz mapper that diffs configured AuthorizationPolicies against the actual service call graph to find peer-authenticated-but-unauthorized edges; test forwarded-identity header trust between sidecars.

trust_assumption: A just-in-time PAM / privileged-access-management system trusts that an elevation request approved through its workflow (justification text, approver click, ticket reference) represents a legitimate, scoped, time-bounded grant, treating the approval event as proof the elevated session will be used as the justification described.
violation: An attacker abuses the elevation workflow itself by self-approving via a controlled or auto-approval path, social-engineering an approver with a plausible justification, racing the activation/deactivation window, or chaining a benign-looking JIT grant into standing privilege, so a procedurally approved elevation becomes attacker-held privilege the system records as legitimate.

techniques.name: JIT elevation self-approval / auto-approval path and approver social engineering
Activation-window race / residual-session abuse after JIT grant expiry

rationale: Organizations replaced standing admin with JIT/PAM elevation (Entra PIM, AWS IAM Identity Center, CyberArk, Teleport) on the premise that approval workflow plus time-boxing equals control. But the workflow trusts the approval event as proof of intent, and the seams are everywhere: auto-approval rules, self-approval in thin teams, approvers rubber-stamping AI-plausible justifications, and sessions/tokens that outlive the JIT window. The system authorizes the approved position, not the actual use, and records the abuse as a legitimate grant, which corrupts the audit trail defenders rely on. There is little tooling that probes a tenant's JIT auto-approval and window-race surface, and detection of post-expiry residual-session abuse is nascent.
suggested_research: Map JIT/PAM products' auto-approval, self-approval, and token-vs-grant lifetime mismatches; build a tester for activation-window races and residual-session persistence after JIT deactivation, and quantify approver susceptibility to LLM-generated justifications.
